Isothermal calorimetry and in-situ XRD will monitor early hydration kinetics. High-resolution techniques (SEM-EDS, TEM, XPS) will characterize the interfacial transition zone between carbonated fines and OPC hydrates. Zeta potential measurements will assess surface charge evolution. Comparative mixes will include uncarbonated fines and systems where carbonated fines are introduced after OPC pre-hydration to decouple physical and chemical effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental mechanistic understanding of how pre-carbonated fines influence cement hydration at the nanoscale. It will provide a scientific basis for optimizing carbonation treatment protocols and mix designs for next-generation low-carbon cement composites, moving beyond empirical approaches.
proposals.novelty: While carbonation of fines is studied for CO2 sequestration, its direct chemical role during OPC hydration is unexplored. This proposal shifts the focus from the bulk modification of fines to the specific reactivity of their carbonated surface with fresh clinker, a novel interfacial materials science perspective.

proposals.title: First-Principles Kinetic Modeling of Carbonate-Binding Phases via High-Throughput Experimentation and Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Carbonation-activated waste-cementitious systems (e.g., using fly ash, steel slag, calcined clay) offer a promising route for CO2 sequestration and low-carbon binder production. However, their performance prediction relies on empirical adaptations of Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) hydration models, which are inadequate as the dominant reaction mechanisms shift from silicate hydration to complex carbonate-aluminate-silicate interactions. A fundamental, mechanism-based understanding is urgently needed to rationally design these materials for durability and tailored properties.
proposals.hypothesis: The reaction kinetics and final phase assemblage in carbonated waste-cementitious systems are governed by a discrete set of non-hydration mechanisms, primarily involving the coupled carbonation of calcium silicates and aluminates, which can be captured by a data-driven model and distilled into a new first-principles kinetic framework.
proposals.method: A high-throughput robotic platform will synthesize a wide matrix of formulations from industrial wastes (steel slag, fly ash) and activators under controlled CO2 exposure. In-situ monitoring via isothermal calorimetry, FTIR, and XRD will generate time-resolved phase evolution data. This multidimensional dataset will train a gradient-boosted regression tree (GBRT) model to identify critical variables controlling reaction rates. The ML-derived relationships will inform the construction of a new kinetic model based on the fundamental principles of nucleation and growth for carbonate phases (e.g., calcite, vaterite, calcium-aluminum-carbonate-hydrates), explicitly excluding OPC hydration paradigms.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first mechanism-based kinetic model specifically for carbonate-dominated binders, moving the field beyond empirical fitting. It will establish a validated high-throughput experimental and data analysis pipeline for rapid formulation screening. The resulting framework will enable the predictive design of carbonated waste-based composites with optimized strength, carbon uptake, and long-term stability.
proposals.novelty: The approach deliberately abandons the OPC hydration conceptual framework to discover the unique chemistry of carbonation-activated systems. It integrates high-throughput in-situ characterization with machine learning not for mere prediction, but explicitly to guide the derivation of a new first-principles kinetic model from the discovered mechanisms.

proposals.title: A Geospatial-Kinetic Modeling Framework for Optimizing the Performance and Supply of Carbonated Waste-Based Supplementary Cementitious Materials
proposals.motivation: The cement industry urgently needs low-carbon supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs), with carbonated industrial wastes (e.g., steel slag, fly ash) showing great promise. However, their large-scale adoption is hindered by two disconnected challenges: variable hydration kinetics that affect in-situ performance, and complex supply-chain logistics that impact economic and environmental viability. Current research treats these as separate silos, creating a gap between lab-scale material development and real-world implementation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by fusing detailed kinetic models of carbonated waste-cement hydration with geospatial supply-chain analysis, we can identify regionally optimal SCM formulations and supply pathways that maximize both technical performance (e.g., strength development) and large-scale adoption potential.
proposals.method: We will first develop and calibrate advanced kinetic models (e.g., extended CEMHYD3D or boundary nucleation and growth models) for specific carbonated waste-cement systems, informed by experimental data from isothermal calorimetry and XRD. Concurrently, we will build a geospatial database of global waste feedstock availability, processing sites, and transportation networks. These will be integrated into a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) optimization framework that minimizes cost and CO2 footprint of the supply chain while constraining outputs to meet performance targets predicted by the kinetic models for given regional blend designs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel decision-support tool that quantitatively links material science with supply-chain logistics, enabling stakeholders to design viable low-carbon concrete blends tailored to regional contexts. It will advance the field by providing a systematic methodology to evaluate and de-risk the commercial-scale deployment of novel carbonated waste SCMs, moving beyond lab-scale property reporting.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of mechanistic hydration kinetics with geospatial-economic supply-chain optimization for carbonated waste SCMs, a fusion not attempted in existing literature which typically addresses these aspects independently.

proposals.title: A Coupled Chemo-Mechanical Acceleration Framework for Predicting the Long-Term Degradation of Calcium-Rich Carbonated Fly Ash-OPC Composite Pastes
proposals.motivation: The development of low-carbon cementitious composites like CRCF-OPC pastes is critical for reducing the environmental impact of construction. However, their long-term durability in aggressive environments (e.g., marine or sulfate-rich soils) remains a major barrier to widespread adoption and standardization. Current testing provides only static 'snapshots' of performance, lacking a predictive model that links specific environmental triggers to the evolution of mechanical properties over time.
proposals.hypothesis: The long-term degradation of CRCF-OPC pastes under combined environmental and mechanical stress can be predicted by a framework that correlates accelerated chemo-mechanical exposure regimes with time-series changes in microstructural phases and mechanical properties. We hypothesize that the carbonated phases in CRCF will exhibit a distinct, quantifiable degradation pathway compared to traditional OPC hydration products.
proposals.method: CRCF-OPC paste samples will be subjected to cyclic exposure regimes combining sulfate (Na2SO4), chloride (NaCl), and carbonation (elevated CO2) solutions with simultaneous mechanical loading (e.g., sustained or cyclic compressive stress). Performance will be monitored through continuous non-destructive testing (ultrasonic pulse velocity, electrical resistivity) and periodic destructive tests (compressive strength, modulus of elasticity, water permeability). Microstructural evolution will be tracked using XRD, TGA, and SEM-EDS at defined intervals to identify the formation and transformation of phases like ettringite, gypsum, and Friedel's salt. The data will be used to develop an empirical-kinetic model linking exposure duration and intensity to property loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, practical framework for predicting the service life of novel carbon-fixing cement composites, directly addressing a key gap in their qualification for durable infrastructure. It will provide fundamental insights into the degradation mechanisms of carbonated waste phases in cementitious systems under coupled stressors, moving beyond binary durability tests.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the coupled application of chemical and mechanical acceleration stresses specifically tailored to CRCF-OPC systems, combined with continuous mechanical monitoring to develop a time-dependent predictive model, rather than a single endpoint measurement. This integrates Poon's expertise in carbonation-activated waste materials with advanced durability testing methodologies.

proposals.title: A Biomimetic Scaling Framework: Tracing the Fate of Carbonation-Activated Kinetics from Paste to Concrete
proposals.motivation: The development of low-carbon, carbon-fixing cementitious systems (e.g., LC3, CSA, carbonated steel slag) is a major pre-2026 research thrust. However, promising paste-level kinetics, such as accelerated setting or strength gain from anhydrous carbonation, often fail to translate predictably to concrete-scale performance, leading to costly trial-and-error in industrial application. A systematic, mechanistic understanding of this scaling failure is critically lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the beneficial kinetic mechanisms observed in carbonation-activated cement pastes are selectively disrupted by the incremental introduction of aggregate interfaces and processing effects, and that these disruption points can be identified and modeled through a staged experimental framework.
proposals.method: We will design a tiered protocol using a model carbonation-activated system (e.g., CSA with CO2 injection). Identical mixes will be tested at three scales: paste, mortar (adding standardized sand), and concrete (adding coarse aggregate and full mixing/placing). At each scale, isothermal calorimetry, in-situ XRD, and SEM-EDS will track hydration/carbonation kinetics and microstructure evolution, with a focus on the interfacial transition zone (ITZ). Statistical analysis of variance (ANOVA) will correlate compositional complexity with deviations from paste-level performance metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalizable, diagnostic framework for scaling novel cementitious chemistries, moving beyond empirical correlation. It will deliver specific knowledge on how aggregate surfaces and mixing water dynamics interact with carbonation reactions, guiding the targeted design of admixtures or mix protocols for reliable industrial translation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct analogical transfer of a biomedical 'translation' problem to construction materials science and the deliberate, incremental addition of systemic complexity to isolate scaling failure mechanisms, a approach not systematically applied in concrete research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Scaling Carbon-Fixing Cementitious Systems: From Paste Kinetics to Mortar and Concrete Performance
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Poon's work, demonstrates that novel carbon-fixing cementitious systems (e.g., using CO2-assisted stirring or ternary blends) show promising kinetic benefits at the paste level, such as accelerated strength development and enhanced carbonation. However, the translation of these benefits to the performance of composite materials containing aggregates (mortar and concrete) remains unverified, creating a critical gap between lab-scale innovation and industrial application. This scale-up is essential for validating the environmental and mechanical advantages of these systems in real-world construction materials.
proposals.hypothesis: The accelerated reaction kinetics and improved microstructural development observed in carbon-fixing cement pastes will directly translate to enhanced early-age mechanical properties and long-term durability in corresponding mortar and concrete composites, without detrimental effects from the inclusion of fine and coarse aggregates.
proposals.method: The research will employ a systematic scaling approach. First, baseline paste compositions from Poon's recent studies (e.g., carbonate-calcined clay cement, cement-fly ash-carbonated steel slag) will be reproduced. These will then be scaled to standard mortar (following ASTM C109/C348) and concrete mixes (following ASTM C192), maintaining constant binder composition and water-to-binder ratio while incorporating standard sand and coarse aggregate. Performance will be evaluated through isothermal calorimetry for kinetics, compressive strength development over time, X-ray diffraction (XRD) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) for phase evolution, and mercury intrusion porosimetry (MIP) for microstructure. Durability indicators, such as resistance to chloride penetration, will also be assessed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset linking the paste-level chemistry of novel carbon-fixing systems to the engineering-scale performance of mortar and concrete. It will establish critical scaling factors and identify potential interfacial transition zone (ITZ) effects, offering practical guidelines for mix design. The results will directly inform the feasibility of industrial adoption by bridging fundamental material science with composite material engineering.
proposals.novelty: While paste-level studies of carbonation-activated and ternary systems are established, a deliberate, systematic investigation scaling these specific carbon-fixing formulations through the full hierarchy of construction materials (paste → mortar → concrete) to validate performance translation is novel and non-obvious, as aggregate inclusion can significantly alter reaction pathways and microstructure.

proposals.title: Quantitative Deconvolution of Isothermal Calorimetry Data for the Rational Design of Clinker-Free, Carbon-Fixing Binders
proposals.motivation: The cement industry urgently needs low-carbon, clinker-free binders. However, novel systems combining aluminosilicate SCMs, activators, and carbonates (e.g., from steel slag) exhibit complex, overlapping reaction peaks in calorimetry, obscuring individual reaction kinetics and synergies. This lack of mechanistic clarity hinders the rational optimization of next-generation binders for performance and carbon sequestration.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced signal deconvolution algorithms can isolate the individual heat flow contributions of distinct reactions (e.g., aluminate activation, silicate dissolution, carbonate formation) within complex SCM blends, enabling the quantitative mapping of reaction kinetics and component synergy as a direct function of mix design.
proposals.method: We will apply and adapt established biochemical deconvolution algorithms, specifically non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) and multivariate curve resolution-alternating least squares (MCR-ALS), to high-resolution isothermal calorimetry data. These algorithms will be trained and validated using calorimetry from well-characterized single-phase and binary model systems (e.g., pure metakaolin, slag, carbonated steel slag). The validated model will then deconvolute heat flow from novel ternary/quaternary blends, correlating isolated peaks with phase development via simultaneous XRD, TGA, and SEM-EDS analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven methodology to decode the 'black box' of complex binder reactions, moving binder design from empirical tuning to targeted engineering. It will deliver quantitative metrics for reaction synergy and kinetics, directly informing the optimization of clinker-free, carbon-fixing cementitious systems for early-age performance and long-term durability.
proposals.novelty: While deconvolution is standard in biochemistry, its application to cement calorimetry is novel. This approach moves beyond qualitative peak assignment to provide a quantitative, component-resolved reaction map for multi-phase SCM-carbonate systems, a critical gap in pre-2026 analytical methods for sustainable cement design.

proposals.title: Chemometric Deconvolution of Surface Interactions in Cement-Carbonated Fines Blends for Predictive Kinetics Modeling
proposals.motivation: The incorporation of carbonated industrial fines (e.g., fly ash, steel slag) into cement is a promising carbon-fixing strategy, but its impact on early-age kinetics and rheology is complex and poorly understood. Current approaches rely on bulk composition analysis, which fails to capture the critical surface-level interactions between fresh OPC particles and the carbonated fines that govern initial hydration and workability. A predictive, mechanistic framework is needed to enable the rational design of these sustainable cementitious systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The unique chemical signature imparted by carbonated fines onto fresh OPC particle surfaces, deconvoluted via multivariate analysis of spectral data, is the primary driver of altered early-age hydration kinetics and rheological properties, enabling the creation of predictive models.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a designed matrix of blends with varying types and dosages of carbonated fines (steel slag, fly ash) in OPC. For each blend, we will collect time-series spectral data (XRD, FTIR, 29Si/27Al NMR) and correlate it with real-time isothermal calorimetry and rheometry. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) will identify latent variables in the spectral data representing surface interactions. Partial Least Squares (PLS) regression will then be used to build quantitative models predicting calorimetric and rheological outputs from the spectral fingerprints.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first diagnostic framework that quantifies the surface-mediated effects of carbonated fines, moving beyond bulk property correlations. It will provide a data-driven model to predict the early-age behavior of novel carbon-fixing cements, accelerating their formulation and industrial adoption. The methodology establishes a new paradigm for analyzing complex interactions in composite cementitious materials.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of chemometrics with cement chemistry to explicitly model surface interactions, rather than bulk composition, represents a significant methodological leap. Applying PLS regression to correlate real-time spectral surface data with kinetics and rheology for carbonated fines is a novel, non-obvious approach to deconvolute synergistic or inhibitory effects.

proposals.title: Mechano-Chemical Activation of Carbonated Recycled Concrete Filler via Pharmaceutical-Inspired Wet Nanomilling: Tailoring Nucleation Sites for Enhanced Cementitious Reactivity
proposals.motivation: The utilization of carbonated recycled concrete filler (CRCF) is a promising route for carbon sequestration and waste valorization in cementitious systems. However, its reactivity as a supplementary cementitious material is often limited. Pre-2026, the field lacks fundamental studies on how the physical processing of CRCF, beyond simple grinding, can engineer its surface properties to control its nucleation behavior and early-age reaction kinetics, which are critical for performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Controlled wet nanomilling of CRCF, analogous to pharmaceutical nanomilling, will generate nanoparticles with fresh, activated surfaces and altered surface chemistry that act as fundamentally different and more effective nucleation sites, accelerating early-stage hydration and pozzolanic reactions compared to conventionally processed CRCF.
proposals.method: CRCF will be produced via accelerated carbonation of recycled concrete paste powder. A high-energy wet bead mill, using zirconia beads and a polycarboxylate-based dispersant as a stabilizer, will be employed to produce nano-CRCF suspensions. The process parameters (milling time, bead size, stabilizer dosage) will be systematically varied. The resulting particles will be characterized for size (DLS, TEM), surface area (BET), and surface energy (inverse gas chromatography). Their nucleation efficiency and reaction kinetics will be studied in model systems (e.g., Ca(OH)2 solution, Portland cement paste) using isothermal calorimetry, in-situ XRD, and SEM from minutes to 72 hours.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel processing-structure-reactivity relationship for CRCF, moving beyond composition to focus on engineered particle surfaces. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of how milling-induced surface states govern nucleation, offering a new lever to design high-performance, low-carbon cementitious composites with precisely controlled early-age properties.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation of pharmaceutical nanomilling principles and stabilizer chemistry to a construction waste material is unprecedented. The focus is not merely on particle size reduction but on intentionally creating and stabilizing specific surface states to manipulate nucleation, a concept not explored in pre-2026 concrete recycling research.

proposals.title: Synergistic Effects of Supplementary Cementitious Materials on Early-Age Fiber-Matrix Bonding and Structural Performance of CRCF-OPC Hybrid Composites
proposals.motivation: The development of sustainable, high-performance fiber-reinforced concrete requires matrices with tailored early-age properties. While Calcium Sulfoaluminate Cement (CRCF)-OPC hybrids offer rapid strength and reduced carbon footprint, their synergy with diverse SCMs for fiber-composite applications is poorly understood. This gap hinders the design of durable, crack-resistant structural elements using industrial by-products and calcined clays, which is critical for advancing low-carbon construction.
proposals.hypothesis: The strategic incorporation of specific SCMs (slag, fly ash, calcined clay) into a CRCF-OPC hybrid matrix will modulate early-age hydration and microstructure, thereby optimizing the fiber-matrix interfacial transition zone (ITZ) and bond-slip behavior, which will directly translate to enhanced crack control and load-bearing capacity in full-scale structural elements.
proposals.method: A systematic experimental program will be conducted. First, paste and mortar mixes with varying SCM types and replacement levels will be characterized for early-age hydration (isothermal calorimetry), rheology, and microstructure (SEM/EDS). Single-fiber pullout tests on steel and synthetic fibers will quantify bond-slip parameters. These results will inform the design of full-scale beam and slab specimens, which will be cast and tested under flexural and shear loads. Performance metrics (cracking, ductility, load capacity) will be correlated with the micro-scale and bond properties to establish predictive relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental, microstructure-informed link between SCM chemistry in hybrid cements and the structural mechanics of fiber-reinforced composites. It will provide a practical design framework for optimizing sustainable, fiber-reinforced concrete using industrial by-products, directly contributing to the reduction of cement clinker content and the advancement of low-carbon, resilient infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: The research uniquely fuses micro-scale fiber-matrix bond investigation with full-scale structural validation within a single CRCF-OPC-SCM system, creating a closed-loop design methodology. It explicitly targets the early-age performance, which is critical for fiber efficiency but rarely studied in such hybrid cement matrices with diverse SCMs.

proposals.title: Stabilizing Phosphogypsum via Accelerated Carbonation: A Novel Pathway for Waste Valorization and Carbon Sequestration
proposals.motivation: Phosphogypsum (PG), a massive by-product of phosphate fertilizer production, presents a severe environmental challenge due to its high acidity, heavy metal content, and radionuclides, with global stockpiles exceeding billions of tons. Concurrently, the cement industry, a major CO2 emitter, urgently needs carbon-negative technologies. Prior work by Poon's group has successfully used accelerated carbonation reactors to carbonate steel slag, enhancing its properties and fixing CO2. This proposal aims to apply this established reactor technology to treat PG, addressing two critical waste streams simultaneously.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treating phosphogypsum in an accelerated carbonation reactor, analogous to methods used for steel slag, will effectively neutralize its acidity, immobilize hazardous constituents, and transform it into a stable, carbonate-rich material suitable for use as a supplementary cementitious material or construction filler.
proposals.method: The research will employ a lab-scale pressurized carbonation reactor system previously developed for steel slag. We will subject raw and pre-treated PG samples to carbonation under controlled conditions of CO2 pressure (0.1-2 MPa), humidity, and temperature. The process will be optimized using a factorial design, varying solid-to-liquid ratio, particle size, and reaction time. The evolution of mineral phases (using XRD, TGA), leaching behavior (via TCLP), microstructure (SEM-EDS), and the degree of carbonation will be systematically characterized to evaluate treatment efficacy and reaction mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel technical pathway for PG management that simultaneously sequesters CO2, transforming a hazardous waste into a benign, value-added material. It will provide fundamental data on the carbonation kinetics and products of PG, contributing to the field of mineral carbonation beyond conventional alkaline wastes. The outcome could lead to a practical, industrial-scale solution for the phosphate industry and a new carbon-fixing feedstock for low-carbon cementitious systems.
proposals.novelty: While carbonation of steel slag and other alkaline industrial wastes is well-studied, the direct application of accelerated reactor-based carbonation specifically to acidic, high-sulfate, and radioactive phosphogypsum is a significant and non-obvious extension of the technology, creating a new synergy between phosphate and cement industries for environmental remediation.

proposals.title: Long-Term Durability and Microstructural Evolution of Anhydrous Carbonated Sulfoaluminate Cement (SAC) in Aggressive Environments
proposals.motivation: Sulfoaluminate cement (SAC) is a promising low-carbon alternative to Portland cement, and recent work has shown that anhydrous carbonation can enhance its early properties and carbon sequestration. However, the long-term durability of carbonated SAC, critical for structural applications, remains largely unexplored. A systematic evaluation of its resistance to common environmental stressors is needed to validate its viability as a sustainable construction material.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the anhydrous carbonation process, which promotes the formation of stable carbonate and aluminate phases, will significantly improve the long-term durability of SAC by densifying the microstructure and reducing the permeability and susceptibility of key phases like ettringite to degradation.
proposals.method: The research will produce carbonated SAC pastes and mortars using the established anhydrous carbonation method with controlled CO2 pressure and duration. Specimens will undergo accelerated durability tests: sulfate resistance (immersion in Na2SO4/MgSO4 solutions), freeze-thaw cycling (ASTM C666), and rapid chloride migration (NT BUILD 492). Performance will be benchmarked against non-carbonated SAC and ordinary Portland cement controls. Microstructural evolution will be characterized using XRD, SEM-EDS, and MIP at regular intervals to correlate phase stability and pore structure changes with macroscopic property loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the long-term durability of carbonated SAC, establishing performance limits for its use in aggressive environments. It will elucidate the microstructural mechanisms governing durability, offering critical insights for optimizing carbonation protocols to enhance both carbon fixation and material longevity.
proposals.novelty: While recent studies focus on the early-age property enhancement of carbonated SAC, this proposal uniquely targets its long-term performance, specifically linking the stability of carbonation-induced phases to resistance against sulfate, freeze-thaw, and chloride attack—a crucial gap for real-world application.

proposals.title: In-situ Synchrotron X-ray Diffraction and Micro-CT Study of Microstructure Evolution during Concurrent Carbonation and Ettringite Formation in Novel Cementitious Systems
proposals.motivation: Accelerated carbonation curing is a promising technology for CO2 sequestration and improving early-age properties of cementitious materials. However, the complex, concurrent reactions involving carbonate precipitation and ettringite formation, especially in systems like sulfoaluminate or ternary cements, are poorly understood at the microstructural level. A mechanistic understanding of these real-time interactions is critical to optimize mix designs for enhanced carbonation efficiency and material performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in-situ carbonation, under controlled humidity and CO2 concentration, will promote a synergistic microstructure where the precipitation of calcium carbonate phases nucleates and stabilizes ettringite formation, leading to a denser, more interconnected pore structure than sequential reactions.
proposals.method: We will employ in-situ synchrotron-based X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a controlled atmosphere cell to track the real-time formation and transformation of phases (e.g., calcite, vaterite, ettringite, monosulfate) in pastes of carbonated steel slag-cement-fly ash and sulfoaluminate cement systems. This will be coupled with in-situ micro-computed tomography (micro-CT) using a custom environmental chamber to visualize 3D microstructural evolution (pore network and crack development) under the same carbonation conditions. Data from both techniques will be temporally correlated to establish process-structure relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, time-resolved evidence of the coupled reaction kinetics and microstructural development during CO2 curing of advanced cementitious systems. It will deliver a validated reaction model to guide the design of industrial carbonatable binders, directly contributing to the development of low-carbon construction materials with improved durability and carbon sequestration capacity.
proposals.novelty: While carbonation or ettringite formation have been studied separately, the simultaneous, in-situ observation of their co-evolution using complementary synchrotron techniques on novel ternary and sulfoaluminate systems is a novel approach to deconvolute their interactive mechanisms.

proposals.title: Enhancing the Kinetics and Extent of CO2 Sequestration in Cementitious Systems via Microwave and Ultrasonic Activation
proposals.motivation: Accelerated carbonation of cementitious materials is a promising route for CO2 sequestration and improving material properties, but conventional methods are often slow or energy-intensive. Pre-2026 research by Poon and others has shown the potential of carbonation in systems like cement-fly ash-steel slag, yet the activation energy barrier for the carbonation reaction remains a limiting factor. Developing low-energy, non-thermal activation methods is crucial for scaling up carbonation technologies for industrial waste valorization and low-carbon concrete production.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted application of microwave or ultrasonic energy fields will selectively activate cement particles and reactive phases (e.g., CaO, C3S, C2S, and aluminate phases), significantly enhancing the rate and total capacity of CO2 uptake during anhydrous carbonation processes compared to conventional mixing or thermal curing.
proposals.method: The research will systematically compare microwave irradiation and ultrasonic cavitation as pre-treatment and in-situ activation methods for ternary blends (e.g., OPC-fly ash-carbonated steel slag) and sulfoaluminate cement. A parametric study will define optimal energy parameters (e.g., microwave power/duration, ultrasonic frequency/power). The carbonation reaction kinetics will be monitored via in-situ thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and XRD, while the microstructural development and final mechanical properties (compressive strength) of the carbonated compacts will be evaluated. The environmental impact will be assessed via life cycle assessment (LCA) focusing on the net carbon footprint.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel processing pathway to intensify carbonation reactions, leading to higher CO2 sequestration yields and faster production cycles for carbon-fixing cementitious materials. It will provide fundamental insights into the mechano-chemical activation mechanisms under energy fields. The outcome will be a practical, energy-efficient protocol that can be integrated into existing industrial processes for manufacturing low-carbon construction products from wastes.
proposals.novelty: While microwave and ultrasound are used in material synthesis, their targeted application to lower the activation energy for the solid-gas carbonation reaction in complex cementitious systems, particularly to simultaneously promote carbonate and aluminate phase reactions, is unexplored. This moves beyond simple mixing or thermal curing to direct particle activation.

proposals.title: Fundamental Surface Kinetics of Anhydrous Carbonation in Sulfoaluminate Cement via Nanoparticle Reaction Platforms
proposals.motivation: Accelerated carbonation of cementitious materials is a promising route for CO2 sequestration and property enhancement, but its fundamental kinetics are obscured by diffusion limitations in bulk pastes. Pre-2026 work, including Poon's recent studies on anhydrous carbonation of sulfoaluminate cement (SAC), has demonstrated improved properties but lacks a mechanistic understanding of the intrinsic surface reaction rates between CO2 and key mineral phases.
proposals.hypothesis: By reducing SAC to nanoparticles, diffusion limitations are minimized, allowing for the direct measurement and modeling of the intrinsic surface reaction kinetics of ye'elimite and other phases with CO2 under anhydrous conditions.
proposals.method: SAC clinker will be ground to nanoparticles (<100 nm) using high-energy planetary ball milling and characterized via XRD, BET, and TEM. Controlled carbonation experiments will be conducted in a thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) system coupled with mass spectrometry, varying CO2 partial pressure, temperature, and humidity. Reaction kinetics will be modeled using established shrinking-core and Avrami-Erofeev models, with data fitting to extract activation energies and intrinsic rate constants. Complementary in-situ FTIR and NMR will probe surface species evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dataset on the intrinsic carbonation kinetics of SAC phases, enabling predictive models for carbonation efficiency. It will establish a fundamental framework to rationally design carbonation processes for other cementitious systems, advancing the science of CO2 utilization in construction materials.
proposals.novelty: While nanoparticle studies exist for OPC, applying this 'scale-down' approach specifically to the complex phase assemblage of SAC to deconvolute surface kinetics from diffusion is novel. It directly builds upon but fundamentally advances Poon's prior bulk-scale work on anhydrous SAC carbonation.

proposals.title: Developing Low-Alkaline Carbon-Fixing Cementitious Systems: A Study on Water-Mediated Carbonation of Non-Traditional Industrial Wastes
proposals.motivation: Current research on CO2 sequestration in cementitious materials heavily focuses on high-alkaline wastes like steel slag or fly ash, which are regionally limited and can pose handling risks. Expanding the portfolio of usable wastes is critical for global, scalable carbon capture and utilization (CCU) in construction. This work addresses the need to decarbonize the built environment by exploring more abundant, lower-pH industrial by-products.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that low-alkaline industrial wastes (e.g., certain non-ferrous slags, recycled concrete fines, or specific mining tailings) can undergo effective water-mediated carbonation, forming stable carbonates and binding phases that enhance the microstructure and mechanical properties of composite cementitious systems.
proposals.method: The research will employ a systematic materials science approach. First, candidate low-alkaline wastes (pH 8-11) will be characterized for mineralogy, reactivity, and fineness. Composite binders will be formulated by partially replacing Portland cement (20-40 wt%) with these wastes. The key intervention is a CO2-assisted vigorous stirring pre-treatment (based on Poon's prior method) in a water slurry, followed by standard mixing, casting, and curing. Specimens will be subjected to accelerated carbonation curing. Performance will be evaluated via compressive strength, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) for carbonate quantification, X-ray diffraction (XRD), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to link phase evolution to microstructure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental understanding of the carbonation mechanisms in low-alkaline waste-cement systems, identifying key reactive phases. It will provide a novel, practical pathway to valorize a broader range of industrial by-products for carbon sequestration, potentially leading to new low-carbon cement standards and more geographically adaptable CCU technologies.
proposals.novelty: While carbonation of alkaline wastes is known, this proposal deliberately challenges the paradigm that high alkalinity is a prerequisite, exploring the mechanistic role of water-mediated carbonation for lower-pH materials that have been largely overlooked for this application.

proposals.title: Development of a Pilot-Scale Continuous Flow Reactor for Accelerated Carbonation of Cementitious Wastes: Process Optimization and Product Characterization
proposals.motivation: The cement industry is a major source of CO2 emissions, and carbonation of cementitious wastes (e.g., recycled concrete fines, steel slag) offers a promising route for permanent CO2 sequestration and upcycling. While batch carbonation processes have been demonstrated at lab-scale, a critical bottleneck for industrial adoption is the lack of scalable, continuous reactor technology that can precisely control the water content—a key parameter for reaction kinetics and product quality—for tonnage-level processing.
proposals.hypothesis: A continuous flow reactor, integrating controlled water mist injection and vigorous mechanical agitation, can maintain the optimal 'damp powder' state for accelerated carbonation, thereby achieving consistent, high-efficiency CO2 uptake and producing a stable, value-added carbonate product from waste cementitious powders.
proposals.method: The project will design and construct a pilot-scale (100 kg/hr capacity) continuous screw or paddle reactor. The system will integrate real-time moisture sensors (NIR or capacitance-based) in a feedback loop with a controlled water mist injection system to maintain a target water content (e.g., 10-20 wt.%). A pre-mixing chamber will ensure uniform CO2 gas distribution (using a simulated flue gas, ~15% CO2). Process parameters (residence time, agitation speed, temperature, CO2 concentration) will be systematically varied using a Design of Experiments (DoE) approach. The carbonated products will be characterized for CO2 uptake (TGA), mineralogy (XRD), and reactivity as a supplementary cementitious material (SCM) via strength activity index tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed engineering design and operational protocol for a continuous carbonation reactor tailored for cementitious wastes, directly addressing the scale-up challenge. It will yield quantitative data on the relationship between continuous process parameters, carbonation efficiency, and the properties of the resulting carbonate-stabilized material, advancing the field from batch laboratory studies toward industrial implementation.
proposals.novelty: While batch carbonation of cement wastes is well-studied, the design of a continuous process with active, real-time water content control for optimal carbonation represents a significant and non-trivial engineering advancement beyond the state-of-the-art, merging process intensification with precise material science conditioning.

proposals.title: Decarbonation Kinetics and Stability of Carbonated Cementitious Phases Under Controlled Hygrothermal Conditions
proposals.motivation: Accelerated carbonation curing is a promising technology for CO2 sequestration and early strength enhancement in cement-based materials, including ternary systems like cement-fly ash-steel slag. However, the long-term stability of the sequestered carbon, primarily as calcium carbonate, under real-world fluctuating humidity and temperature is poorly understood. A critical knowledge gap exists regarding the potential for the reverse reaction (decarbonation), which could compromise durability and negate carbon benefits, limiting the reliable deployment of this technology.
proposals.hypothesis: The stability of carbonates formed in carbonated cement matrices is governed by a critical relative humidity threshold and temperature, beyond which decarbonation reactions become thermodynamically favorable and kinetically significant, with the reaction rate and extent dependent on the carbonate polymorph and the surrounding binder chemistry.
proposals.method: The study will fabricate carbonated paste samples from sulfoaluminate cement and ternary cement-fly ash-steel slag systems using established CO2-assisted vigorous stirring and anhydrous carbonation methods. Carbonated specimens will be subjected to controlled environments in climatic chambers, varying relative humidity (30-95%) and temperature (20-60°C) over extended periods. Phase stability will be monitored via in-situ and ex-situ techniques: quantitative X-ray diffraction (QXRD) with Rietveld refinement to track carbonate and hydrate phase changes, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) for mass loss associated with decarbonation, and micro-Raman spectroscopy for localized chemical mapping. Reaction kinetics will be modeled using the Johnson-Mehl-Avrami-Kolmogorov (JMAK) equation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic dataset on decarbonation boundaries for carbonated industrial byproduct-cement systems, establishing safe operational envelopes for their use. It will deliver a predictive kinetic model for carbonate stability, directly informing lifecycle assessments and durability specifications for carbon-cured concrete, thereby de-risking its adoption for sustainable construction.
proposals.novelty: While extensive pre-2026 research focuses on carbonation *uptake*, this proposal uniquely investigates the *inverse problem* of CO2 release. It applies controlled hygrothermal aging, common in durability studies, to explicitly challenge the permanence of carbon sequestration in novel cementitious systems, a critical but overlooked aspect for their environmental claim validation.

proposals.title: Enhancing Direct Air Capture Kinetics and Capacity via Optimally Hydrated Waste Cementitious Sorbents
proposals.motivation: Direct Air Capture (DAC) is critical for mitigating climate change but faces challenges with high energy costs and sorbent material limitations. Concurrently, the cement industry generates vast amounts of waste cementitious fines (e.g., from recycled concrete) and is a major CO2 emitter. Developing low-cost, reactive sorbents from industrial waste streams presents a dual opportunity for carbon-negative construction materials and improved DAC economics.
proposals.hypothesis: That the controlled hydration of waste cement powders (from cement, fly ash, or carbonated steel slag) can create a metastable, highly reactive calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) and portlandite matrix, which will exhibit significantly enhanced kinetics and capacity for atmospheric CO2 uptake compared to conventional sorbents or untreated wastes.
proposals.method: We will produce sorbents by subjecting finely ground waste cement and blended systems (e.g., cement-fly ash) to controlled hydration regimes (varying water-to-solid ratio, duration, and curing temperature) to optimize the reactive phase assemblage. The CO2 capture performance will be evaluated using a fixed-bed reactor under simulated DAC conditions (400-500 ppm CO2, 25-80% RH). We will characterize the sorbents pre- and post-carbonation using XRD, TGA, and SEM to correlate phase evolution with uptake rates and capacities, identifying the optimal hydration parameters for DAC enhancement.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel material pathway for DAC by creating high-performance sorbents from abundant waste streams, directly linking the construction materials and carbon capture fields. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how the nano/micro-structure of hydrated cement wastes influences their carbonation reactivity with dilute CO2 streams, guiding the design of next-generation, low-cost sorbents.
proposals.novelty: While carbonation of cementitious materials is well-studied for curing or aggregate treatment, its deliberate engineering as a sorbent for *direct air capture* is unexplored. The novelty lies in tuning the hydration state specifically to maximize surface area and reactive site density for atmospheric CO2, rather than for strength development.

ideas.title: Early-age fiber-matrix bonding and structural performance of full-scale CRCF-OPC composites with diverse SCMs
ideas.description: This research investigates the synergistic effects of incorporating diverse supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) into a Calcium Sulfoaluminate Cement - Ordinary Portland Cement (CRCF-OPC) hybrid matrix used for fiber-reinforced composites. The core objective is to systematically evaluate how different SCMs (e.g., slag, fly ash, calcined clays) influence the early-age hydration, rheology, and microstructure of the matrix, thereby controlling the fundamental bonding and stress-transfer mechanisms with steel, synthetic, or natural fibers at the micro-scale. These paste- and fiber-scale investigations are directly coupled with the casting and testing of full-scale structural concrete elements (e.g., beams, slabs) using the optimized composite mixes. The fusion creates a closed-loop validation framework that directly links chemical and microstructural modifications induced by SCMs to both the early-age development of composite action and the ultimate structural performance, providing a fundamental yet practical design methodology for next-generation, sustainable, fiber-reinforced cementitious composites.

ideas.title: Decoupled Carbonation: A Composite Shell-Core System for Durable SCM-Concrete Structures
ideas.description: This research redesigns the use of carbonation by separating its beneficial and detrimental effects into distinct material zones within a single structural element. It develops a prefabricated or cast-in-place shell made of ultra-high SCM concrete designed for optimized strength gain via accelerated carbonation curing. This carbonated shell then serves as a permanent, protective formwork for a cast-in-place conventional reinforced concrete core, which maintains a high alkaline environment to protect steel. The system directly addresses the corrosion flaw by using the carbonated layer's reduced permeability as a barrier, turning carbonation from a durability threat into a manufactured protective asset for the reinforced core.

ideas.title: SCM-Dominant Binder Systems with a Minimal, Engineered Activation Nucleus
ideas.description: This research proposes a paradigm shift from clinker replacement to clinker functionalization, aiming to develop new binder systems where Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs) constitute over 90% of the solid mass. The core innovation is the design of a minimal, tailored 'activation nucleus'—a precisely formulated chemical or mineral component that reliably initiates and governs the hydration and pozzolanic reactions of the SCM matrix. The system will be engineered for tunable performance, allowing setting time and early strength development to be dialed in independently of clinker content. This approach targets the robustness and code-acceptance of traditional systems while drastically reducing the carbon footprint associated with the binder fraction.

ideas.title: Mechanism Discovery and First-Principles Modeling for Carbonated Waste-Cementitious Systems
ideas.description: This research abandons the adaptation of OPC hydration frameworks and instead focuses on discovering the fundamental reaction mechanisms unique to carbonated waste-cementitious blends. A high-throughput experimental platform synthesizes and monitors a wide matrix of CRCF formulations using in-situ techniques like FTIR, XRD, and calorimetry to capture real-time phase evolution. The resulting multidimensional dataset trains a machine learning model to identify the key chemical and microstructural variables controlling reaction kinetics. Finally, these insights are used to construct new, mechanism-based kinetic models from first principles, specifically for carbonate-dominated binding systems, ensuring predictive accuracy for performance and durability.

ideas.title: In Situ AFM Probing of Carbonated Fines' Interfacial Nucleation and Chemical Communication with Fresh OPC
ideas.description: This research direction combines the specific chemical hypothesis of Idea A with the nanoscale measurement capability of Idea B to create a novel in situ investigation. Using atomic force microscopy (AFM) in a controlled humidity cell, the experiment will directly probe the interface between a fresh ordinary Portland cement (OPC) clinker surface and dispersed carbonated recycled concrete fines (CRCF). The synergy allows for the simultaneous measurement of nucleation forces, topographical changes, and adhesion chemistry on the OPC surface in the presence of the carbonated fines, providing definitive evidence for or against surface alteration. This approach can distinguish between physical seeding effects and true chemical interaction at the OPC interface, a distinction impossible with bulk techniques alone.

ideas.title: Field-Validated SCM Blends Engineered for Controlled Reactivity in Structural Concrete
ideas.description: This research program systematically tests and validates the performance of engineered supplementary cementitious material (SCM) blends in full-scale structural elements, where the reactivity profiles are intentionally designed rather than simply maximized. It investigates how blending high-reactivity SCMs (e.g., silica fume) with slower-reactivity or latent materials (e.g., some natural pozzolans, specific slags) can optimize the hydration kinetics for real-world structural needs, such as mitigating early-age thermal cracking in mass pours or ensuring sustained long-term strength gain. The core novelty is the field validation of these reactivity-tailored blends in actual structural components (beams, columns, slabs) to correlate controlled chemical kinetics with measurable engineering outcomes like crack development, deflection, and in-situ strength evolution. This bridges the gap between material science reactivity theory and the holistic, time-dependent performance requirements of structural engineering practice.

ideas.title: Designing Co-Evolved Binder-Aggregate Systems from Recycled Components for Clinker-Free Concrete
ideas.description: This research direction proposes the co-design of novel, clinker-free binder systems and recycled aggregate treatments as a single, integrated material system. Instead of using SCMs merely to replace cement in a standard mix, they are engineered to form binders that chemically activate or physically seal the porous, cracked surfaces of recycled concrete aggregates. This holistic approach aims to transform the aggregate-binder interface from a performance liability into a tailored zone of enhanced durability and strength. The ultimate goal is to create a new class of concrete where both the binder and the aggregate are derived from industrial by-products and construction waste, achieving performance parity with conventional concrete through designed synergy rather than component optimization in isolation.

ideas.title: Supply-Chain-Aware Kinetic Models for Carbonated Waste-SCM Blends
ideas.description: This research develops integrated kinetic-supply chain models for novel supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) derived from carbonated industrial wastes. It fuses advanced kinetic hydration models, specific to carbonated waste-cement systems, with geospatial and economic analysis of the material's global supply chains. The synergy enables the prediction of how regional variations in feedstock availability, processing, and transportation will impact the in-situ performance and ultimate sustainability of these blends. The outcome is a decision-support tool that identifies optimal SCM formulations and supply pathways to maximize both technical performance and large-scale adoption viability.

ideas.title: Multivariate Chemometric Modeling of Carbonated Fines' Surface Interactions for Predictive Cement Kinetics
ideas.description: This research fuses chemometrics with a specific carbonation hypothesis to develop a novel diagnostic and predictive framework for cementitious systems. It applies multivariate analysis (PCA, PLS) to spectral and microstructural data (e.g., from XRD, FTIR, NMR) to deconvolute and quantify the unique chemical signature imparted by carbonated fines onto fresh OPC particle surfaces. The core synergy is using these chemometric models to correlate the evolving surface chemistry, not just bulk composition, with real-time kinetics (calorimetry) and rheological properties across a designed experimental space. This approach can reveal hidden interaction effects and create predictive models for how pre-carbonated fines alter early-age behavior, moving beyond observation to mechanistic, data-driven control.

ideas.title: From In-Vitro Efficacy to In-Vivo Performance: A Biomaterials-Inspired Framework for Scaling Cementitious Kinetics
ideas.description: This research idea applies an analogical framework from biomedical engineering, where translating in-vitro drug efficacy to in-vivo performance is a critical challenge. The analogous solution involves a staged, incremental introduction of systemic complexity to pinpoint where and why the initial beneficial mechanism fails. Adapted to concrete science, the idea is to systematically evaluate if paste-level kinetic benefits (e.g., faster setting, higher early strength) translate to mortar and concrete performance. This will be done by designing a tiered testing protocol that adds compositional complexity stepwise—from paste to mortar (adding fine aggregate) to concrete (adding coarse aggregate and full processing). At each scale, matched analytical methods will track the evolution of hydration kinetics, microstructure, and interface properties to identify the specific mechanisms (e.g., adsorption on aggregate surfaces, altered water availability, ITZ defects) that enhance, diminish, or negate the initial paste-level benefit, providing a predictive framework for scaling admixture performance.

ideas.title: Coupled Chemo-Mechanical Acceleration Framework for Predicting Long-Term Degradation of CRCF-OPC Composite Pastes
ideas.description: This research establishes a predictive framework for the long-term performance of Calcium-Rich Carbonated Fly Ash (CRCF)-OPC pastes by integrating accelerated environmental exposure with continuous mechanical and microstructural monitoring. The study subjects samples to cyclic regimes of chemical attack (sulfate, chloride, carbonation) combined with mechanical loading to simulate decades of service in aggressive environments. Performance is evaluated through time-series measurements of compressive strength, modulus, and permeability, correlated with periodic microstructural analysis to identify degradation pathways. The outcome is a validated model that predicts service life by linking specific environmental triggers to the evolution of mechanical properties and durability indices, moving beyond a simple snapshot of 'long-term' properties.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmaceutical Nanomilling for Nano-CRCF Particle Nucleation Studies
ideas.description: This research seed is expanded via analogy to pharmaceutical nanomilling. In drug formulation, nanomilling is used to create stable nanoparticle suspensions of insoluble compounds, drastically altering their dissolution kinetics and bioavailability. The analogous solution involves precise mechanical processing with stabilizers. Adapted to materials science, we propose using a similar top-down wet milling approach to produce nano-sized carbonated recycled concrete filler (CRCF) particles, intentionally engineering their surface state. The core investigation will be whether this controlled nano-scaling process, which introduces fresh surfaces and mechanical activation, yields fundamentally different nucleation sites and reaction kinetics compared to conventionally ground CRCF when used as a supplementary cementitious material or in other chemical processes, focusing on the link between milling-induced surface properties and early-stage reaction mechanisms.

ideas.title: Deconvolution calorimetry for next-generation SCM binder design
ideas.description: This research applies advanced signal deconvolution algorithms from biochemical calorimetry to the isothermal calorimetry of novel supplementary cementitious material (SCM) blends designed to replace Portland cement clinker entirely. The technique isolates overlapping heat flow peaks from complex, multi-phase reactions—such as those between aluminosilicates, activators, and carbonates—that are otherwise indistinguishable in conventional analysis. This allows researchers to quantitatively map the contribution, kinetics, and synergy of each reaction component within a new binder system. Consequently, it provides a powerful, data-driven tool to rationally design and optimize clinker-free cement alternatives by targeting specific, desirable reaction products and suppressing undesirable ones.

ideas.title: Accelerated Decoupling and Predictive Modeling of Degradation Pathways in Novel SCM-Concrete Systems
ideas.description: This research develops and validates an accelerated, mechanistic framework to predict the decades-long durability of concrete with new supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs). The approach systematically subjects blends to coupled chemo-mechanical stressors in controlled environments to isolate and quantify dominant degradation pathways like sulfate attack, ASR, and chloride ingress. Microstructural and chemical evolution is tracked in situ to build physics-based models that correlate accelerated test results with long-term field performance. The outcome is a validated predictive toolkit that enables robust durability assessment within a 2-3 year timeline, de-risking the adoption of novel SCMs.

ideas.title: Designing Clinker-Free Binder Systems via Phase Field Theory and Nucleation Catalysis
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs) must react with Portland cement clinker. Drawing an analogy from metallurgy, where entirely new alloys were designed by predicting and catalyzing multi-phase microstructures from elemental powders, this idea proposes a method to create clinker-free binders. The adapted approach involves using computational phase field and thermodynamic modeling to predict stable hydrate phases from SCM chemistries, then employing precisely designed nucleation catalysts (e.g., specific alkalis or seed crystals) to directly synthesize these novel, high-performance binder microstructures during the SCM reaction, thereby creating a completely new class of cementitious materials without any Portland cement clinker.

ideas.title: Strategic SCM portfolio optimization for global supply chains based on reactivity kinetics
ideas.description: This research integrates global supply chain modeling with the nuanced understanding that not all concrete applications require high early pozzolanic reactivity. It develops a framework to evaluate and map the global availability of diverse SCMs—from highly reactive metakaolin to slower-reactive natural pozzolans or certain slags—against regional construction practices, transportation networks, and climate-specific durability needs. The core innovation is a decision-support model that optimizes the SCM supply chain not just for cost and carbon, but for engineered reactivity, enabling the strategic deployment of slower-reacting materials where they provide long-term strength and crack-resistance benefits, thereby improving lifecycle performance and reducing waste in the built environment.

ideas.title: Correlative In-Situ Raman Spectroscopy and 4D Micro-Tomography for Spatiotemporally Resolved Hydration-Structure Evolution
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel correlative in-situ platform that integrates time-resolved Raman spectroscopy with fast, synchrotron-based X-ray micro-tomography. The same cementitious sample is monitored simultaneously, with Raman providing chemical identification and quantification of phases like C-S-H, portlandite, and ettringite, while micro-tomography captures the 4D (3D + time) evolution of porosity, microcracks, and phase distribution. This allows for the creation of spatiotemporal maps that directly link the kinetics of a specific chemical reaction front to the nucleation and growth of the surrounding pore structure, moving beyond parallel observation to establish mechanistic causality. The resulting dataset will validate and refine microstructural evolution models by providing unprecedented experimental constraints on the chemical drivers of physical property development.

ideas.title: In-situ Microstructural Tracking of SCM-Recycled Aggregate Interfacial Zone Evolution
ideas.description: This research employs advanced in-situ imaging techniques, such as SEM and X-ray microtomography, to track the temporal evolution of the pore structure and hydration products specifically within the interfacial zones of recycled aggregates. By systematically varying SCM type and dosage, the study directly links SCM chemistry to the microstructural development and bonding at this vulnerable region. This provides a fundamental, mechanistic understanding of how SCMs can be tailored to heal defects in recycled aggregate surfaces, transforming a performance liability into an engineered component. The outcome is a data-driven framework for the co-design of SCM and recycled aggregate systems, optimizing both sustainability and durability from the microscale up.

ideas.title: Deconvoluting the Energetic Signature of Carbonation for Precise Carbon Footprint Accounting
ideas.description: This research applies advanced calorimetric signal processing and deconvolution techniques, adapted from biochemistry, to the exothermic carbonation reactions of waste materials (e.g., steel slag, concrete fines). By isolating the specific heat flow signature of the carbonation reaction from overlapping thermal signals of hydration, drying, or other concurrent processes, it becomes possible to directly and accurately measure the reaction energy. This energy-resolved data is then used to refine the net carbon footprint calculation, moving beyond average energy allocations to attribute energy use (and its associated emissions) specifically to the carbon sequestration step, thereby validating or challenging the claim that sustainability benefits are solely from waste incorporation.

ideas.title: Closed-loop, data-constrained reactive transport modeling for CRCF-OPC hydration
ideas.description: This research direction integrates in-situ Raman spectroscopy directly into a geochemical reactive transport modeling workflow for Calcium-Rich Carbonation-Cured Fly Ash - Ordinary Portland Cement (CRCF-OPC) systems. The spectroscopic data, capturing real-time formation of carbonates, silicates, and other species, is used to constrain model parameters and initial conditions, reducing uncertainty. The calibrated model can then simulate ion mobility, precipitation sequences, and local saturation indices at a mechanistic level, predicting evolution beyond the experimental observation window. This synergy creates a powerful tool for designing and optimizing CRCF-OPC mixes by linking observable chemical fingerprints to underlying transport and reaction dynamics.

ideas.title: Heterogeneity-robust carbonation of concrete fines via integrated characterization and adaptive reactor control
ideas.description: This system addresses the fatal flaw of feedstock variability by designing an integrated industrial process that begins with automated characterization and conditioning of incoming demolition fines. The conditioned material is then fed into a modular, continuously stirred reactor where carbonation conditions are dynamically optimized in real-time based on the characterized feed properties. This ensures maximum and consistent carbonation efficiency across heterogeneous waste streams, producing a reliable, specification-grade recycled carbonate aggregate. The core innovation is the closed-loop feedback between feedstock analysis and reactor control, transforming a batch-sensitive chemical process into a robust, continuous industrial operation.

ideas.title: System-Level Life Cycle Assessment of Carbonated Waste Materials: Quantifying Net Sustainability Benefits and Burden-Shifting Risks
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple carbon footprint of the carbonation process to conduct a full life cycle assessment (LCA) of construction materials incorporating carbonated waste. The system quantifies the net environmental impacts across multiple categories—including global warming potential, resource use, and human toxicity—from raw material extraction to end-of-life. It explicitly models and tests for burden-shifting, such as increased transportation emissions or reduced product longevity, to determine the true, system-wide sustainability benefit. The outcome is a robust, decision-support model that identifies optimal scenarios where waste carbonation delivers genuine, net-positive environmental outcomes without unintended consequences.

ideas.title: Mapping Polymer Additive Effects on Cement Hydration via Pharmaceutical Drug-Receptor Interaction Models
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methodologies from pharmaceutical biochemistry, where the effects of small-molecule drugs on protein kinetics are precisely characterized, to the study of chemical admixtures in CRCF-OPC paste. The analogous problem is understanding how a molecule (drug/admixture) alters the kinetics of a complex reaction (enzyme function/cement hydration). The pharmaceutical field solves this using techniques like isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and stopped-flow spectroscopy to determine binding mechanisms and kinetic parameters. Adapted to cement chemistry, the idea is to employ microcalorimetry (analogous to ITC) and rapid-setting rheometry (analogous to stopped-flow) to systematically map how superplasticizers or accelerators adsorb onto or interact with specific cement phases (e.g., C3S, C-S-H nuclei). The goal is to quantify interaction energetics and categorize the admixture's mechanism—such as competitive surface site blocking, non-competitive alteration of nucleation barriers, or allosteric-like modification of solution chemistry—thereby providing a more fundamental, mechanistic understanding of early hydration kinetics beyond traditional cumulative heat measurement.

ideas.title: In-situ AFM investigation of nanoscale aging mechanisms in CRCF-OPC pastes for long-term property prediction
ideas.description: This research combines in-situ atomic force microscopy (AFM) with long-term mechanical testing to establish a fundamental, mechanistic link between nanoscale interfacial processes and the macroscopic aging of CRCF-OPC pastes. By using AFM to periodically measure nanoscale adhesion, stiffness, and nucleation phenomena at the CRCF particle interface within a hydrating and aging cement matrix, the study will track how these nanoscale properties evolve over time. This data will be directly correlated with concurrent measurements of compressive strength, shrinkage, and chemical durability (e.g., chloride ingress) on companion bulk samples. The synergy creates a predictive framework where early-time nanoscale signatures can forecast long-term performance, offering a new paradigm for designing durable, low-carbon cementitious composites.

ideas.title: Apply in-situ Raman spectroscopy with microfluidic confinement for early hydration tracking
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from biochemistry—using microfluidic devices to confine and analyze small biological samples in real-time—to the problem of tracking early chemical species formation during cement hydration. In the analogous field, microfluidics enables precise environmental control and high-resolution spectroscopic monitoring of processes like single-cell metabolism. The adapted research idea involves designing a specialized microfluidic cell to confine a minimal volume of cement paste or a single particle, creating a controlled hydration environment. Applying in-situ Raman spectroscopy through this cell allows for the real-time, spatially resolved tracking of early hydration products (e.g., ettringite, portlandite, C-S-H nuclei) with reduced signal interference, providing unprecedented detail on the initial chemical evolution of cement.

ideas.title: Investigate long-term mechanical properties of CRCF-OPC pastes using polymer aging and creep modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from polymer science to investigate the long-term mechanical properties of CRCF-OPC pastes. In polymer engineering, the long-term performance and creep of materials are predicted using accelerated aging tests and time-temperature superposition principles to model behavior over extended periods. By applying this analogous approach, the study will subject CRCF-OPC paste specimens to a range of controlled, elevated temperature and humidity environments to accelerate post-hydration chemical processes and microstructural changes. The compressive strength, modulus, and creep deformation data collected from these accelerated conditions will be analyzed to construct master curves and predictive models. This will allow for the extrapolation of the material's compressive strength evolution and durability—including resistance to deformation under sustained load—over multi-decade timescales relevant to infrastructure service life, providing critical data for the long-term viability of this novel cementitious system.

ideas.title: A Phase IV Clinical Trial Framework for Supplementary Cementitious Materials
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Phase IV clinical trial framework from pharmaceutical development to the validation of supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) in concrete. In pharmaceuticals, Phase IV trials monitor long-term real-world efficacy and safety after initial approval. The adapted research idea involves creating a structured, multi-site deployment and monitoring program for novel SCM-concrete mixes. By instrumenting a diverse portfolio of full-scale structural elements (like bridge decks, building frames, or marine pilings) with sensors and implementing a long-term sampling protocol, the research would generate validated, real-world data on durability, mechanical performance, and sustainability benefits under varied climatic and loading conditions, moving beyond controlled lab tests to definitive proof of performance in service.

ideas.title: Applying Bioremediation's 'Consortium' Approach to SCM Development
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that SCMs must be pure and consistent by adapting a solution from environmental bioremediation. In that field, complex, contaminated sites are cleaned using microbial consortia, not single strains, leveraging functional diversity for robustness. Adapted to cement research, the proposal is to intentionally formulate and test defined 'SCM consortia' from multiple, variable industrial by-products (e.g., blending a high-alumina fly ash with a high-silica slag and a contaminated clay). The goal is to design mixes where the chemical and physical interactions between the different impure components neutralize detrimental effects or enhance reactivity, creating a predictable and high-performance composite binder directly from heterogeneous, real-world waste streams, bypassing the need for costly purification.

ideas.title: In-situ AFM nanomechanical mapping of SCM-concrete interfaces in full-scale structural elements
ideas.description: This research direction employs atomic force microscopy (AFM) to perform in-situ nanomechanical mapping and force measurements directly on the interfacial transition zones (ITZs) and cement hydration products within samples extracted from full-scale structural concrete elements incorporating various supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs). By correlating nanoscale properties—such as adhesion, modulus, and nucleation kinetics at SCM particle surfaces—with the measured macro-scale engineering performance (e.g., strength, permeability, cracking) of the same structural elements over time, a fundamental, mechanistic link is established. This approach moves beyond validating bulk performance to actively diagnosing and predicting the durability of SCM-concrete systems based on their nanoscale interfacial characteristics, guiding the optimization of next-generation, high-performance sustainable concretes.

ideas.title: Life Cycle Assessment of Carbonated Construction Materials: A Metabolic Network Analysis
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that the sustainability benefit of carbonated construction materials comes solely from waste incorporation and CO2 sequestration. By drawing an analogy from Systems Biology and Metabolic Engineering—specifically Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) used to model metabolic networks—the idea adapts a holistic, constraint-based modeling approach. The adapted research proposes to model the material's lifecycle as a network of energy and mass fluxes. This system-level analysis would quantify the true net carbon footprint by balancing all 'metabolic' costs (energy for crushing, carbonation process energy, transport) against the sequestration 'yield' from waste, moving beyond a simple benefit attribution to identify the process conditions and system boundaries where net carbon negativity is genuinely achieved.

ideas.title: In-situ 4D Tomographic Imaging of Cement Hydration Dynamics
ideas.description: This research employs in-situ synchrotron X-ray microtomography within a controlled hydration cell to capture continuous, three-dimensional image sequences of microstructure evolution during cement hydration. By acquiring volumetric data at high temporal resolution, the system directly quantifies the dynamic processes of phase dissolution, precipitation, and pore space transformation as they happen. This 4D dataset is synchronized with real-time isothermal calorimetry and aqueous chemistry measurements, enabling the direct mechanistic linkage of observed physical changes to the underlying chemical kinetics and heat flow. The approach moves beyond static correlation to provide a causal, process-oriented model of microstructure development.

ideas.title: Climate-Responsive Hydration Tuning via SCM Blends
ideas.description: This research direction seeks to establish causal links between the precisely controlled early hydration kinetics of tailored SCM blends and the development of climate-specific durable microstructures. By decoupling and then re-coupling early reaction pathways (e.g., nucleation, pore solution chemistry) with long-term outcomes, we can design blends where the initial kinetics act as a programmed 'signal' that dictates the subsequent formation of a sulfate-resistant, low-permeability, or frost-durable matrix. The synergy allows for the optimization of SCM combinations not just for their chemical composition, but for their kinetic performance under specific temperature and humidity regimes, enabling materials that self-adapt their early-stage reactions to meet the long-term demands of marine, freeze-thaw, or arid environments.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Predictive Framework for Novel SCM Evaluation and Integration
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic, multi-tiered evaluation framework for novel supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) that moves beyond rigid standardization. It combines advanced material characterization, accelerated degradation tests, and predictive machine learning models to estimate long-term performance from short-term data, reducing the time and cost barriers for new materials. The framework establishes provisional performance categories, enabling the safe use of promising SCMs in specified applications while real-world monitoring data is collected. This adaptive system is designed to continuously integrate new findings and material types, fostering innovation while managing risk through evidence-based, evolving guidelines rather than static standards.

ideas.title: Geospatial Supply Chain Modeling for Real-World, Variable-Property SCMs
ideas.description: This research develops a geospatial supply chain and techno-economic model that explicitly incorporates the inherent chemical and physical variability of real-world industrial by-products (e.g., slags, ashes, tailings) as potential supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs). Instead of assuming uniform, lab-grade inputs, the model treats material properties as probabilistic distributions based on source industry and location, directly impacting processing requirements, transport costs, and final concrete performance. This enables the identification of regionally optimized SCM deployment strategies that match variable waste streams with local cement demand, quantifying the true economic and environmental viability of circular economy pathways for the global construction industry.
